original_abstract: It is widely recognized that the biological interactions of nanoparticles (NPs) are dictated by the "protein corona"—the layer of serum proteins that spontaneously adsorbs onto the NP surface. While it is established that the corona’s composition depends on the biological media, we hypothesized that the unique physiological changes associated with specific diseases would result in distinct, disease-specific coronas. To investigate this, we incubated polystyrene and silica nanoparticles with plasma samples obtained from donors across a broad spectrum of medical conditions, including breast cancer, diabetes, rheumatism, hypercholesterolemia, and pregnancy, among others. Our findings reveal that the specific underlying disease or physiological state significantly alters the protein fingerprint of the corona. Consequently, we introduce the concept of the "personalized protein corona" (PPC) as a critical factor in nanomedicine. These results suggest that future diagnostic and therapeutic nanocarriers must be tailored to the PPC/individual patient profile to ensure clinical efficacy and safety.
humanized_abstract: The biological interactions of nanoparticles (NPs) are supposed to be mediated by the “protein corona” which is the layer of serum proteins that spontaneously adheres to the surface of NPs. It is well established that the composition of the corona is dependent on the biological media, whereas we investigated the influence of the physiological changes occurring during different diseases and demonstrated the formation of a “disease-specific corona”. To this end, we applied polystyrene and silica NPs in studies with human plasma samples from healthy donors and patients suffering from various diseases including breast cancer, diabetes, rheumatism, hypercholesterolemia, pregnancy etc. These investigations clearly demonstrated that a range of different diseases and physiological states lead to totally different protein fingerprint of the protein corona which leads to the introduction of the term “personalized protein corona“ (PPC) in the context of nanomedicine. Thus, any future designed diagnostic and/or therapeutic nanocarrier will necessarily need to take into account the PPC and the corresponding individual patient’s profile for assuring the expected effect of nanomedicine.

Thus, any future designed diagnostic and/or therapeutic nanocarrier will necessarily need to take into account the PPC and the corresponding individual patient’s profile for assuring the expected effect of nanomedicine.
document.input: It is widely recognized that the biological interactions of nanoparticles (NPs) are dictated by the "protein corona"—the layer of serum proteins that spontaneously adsorbs onto the NP surface. While it is established that the corona’s composition depends on the biological media, we hypothesized that the unique physiological changes associated with specific diseases would result in distinct, disease-specific coronas. Our findings reveal that the specific underlying disease or physiological state significantly alters the protein fingerprint of the corona. Consequently, we introduce the concept of the "personalized protein corona" (PPC) as a critical factor in nanomedicine. These results suggest that future diagnostic and therapeutic nanocarriers must be tailored to the PPC/individual patient profile to ensure clinical efficacy and safety.
